coredata.dc:title: Highly fluorescent carbon dots from peanut shells as potential probes for copper ion: The optimization and analysis of the synthetic process
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Xiaohong
Dong, Yuanhua
Sun, Hanyuan
Chen, Ningsheng
coredata.dc:description: The use of natural materials, a renewable resource, instead of chemicals as carbon precursors for simple synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots (FCDs) remains a significant challenge. Here, we report the preparation of FCDs with a photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield (QY) of 10.58% from peanut shells via one-pot pyrolysis treatment optimized by using a central composite experiment design. Optimum pyrolysis conditions were found to be 400 °C temperature, 4 h duration, and 70 g peanut shell weight. The as-prepared FCDs possess unique excitation-dependent behavior, good water dispersibility and high photostability. The results of Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy to analyze the pyrolytic process indicated the complete combustion of peanut shells happened at 3 and 4 h at 400 °C. The PL intensity of the FCDs was not always proportional to the corresponding QY value in our work due to the different amount of carbon-rich residues after the pyrolysis process. Fluorescence-quenching trials were conducted to analyze their sensitivity and selectivity in Cu2+ detection. The detection limit was found to be 4.8 μM. Our pyrolysis treatment of peanut shells for preparing FCDs is not only a green and facile method but also a means of recycling peanut shells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence carbon dots
Photoluminescence
Sensing
Wastes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: pH adjustment of FCD aqueous solutions
Effect of pyrolysis conditions on the QY of FCDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis route of fluorescent carbon dots.
Predicted versus observed plot for quantum yields of fluorescent carbon dots.

Three-dimensional response surface plots of quantum yield as a function: (a) pyrolysis time and temperature, (b) peanut shell weight and pyrolysis temperature and (c) peanut shell weight and pyrolysis time.

(a) UV-vis absorption spectra (red line) and PL spectra for prepared FCDs derived from peanut shell (blue line). Inset: digital photographs of as-prepared solution of FCDs (left) and the same solution shown under light irradiation at 312 nm (right). (b) wavelength-dependent fluorescent emission spectra of FCDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) Low-magnified, (b) high-magnified TEM images, (c) the diameter distribution of the FCDs and (d) XRD pattern of the FCDs.

(a) XPS survey spectrum of FCDs, (b) the high-resolution C1s peaks and the fitting curves, (c) the high-resolution O1s peaks and the fitting curves and FTIR absorption spectra of (d) peanut shells, of peanut shells treated at 400 °C for (e) 1 h, (f) 2 h, (g) 3 h, (h) 4 h, and of (i) synthesized FCDs.

(a) The stability of FCDs in different temperature ranging from −4 °C to 60 °C, (b) fluorescence variation of FCDs in different pH solutions, (c) the stability of FCDs and Rhodamine B under continuous UV irradiation for 120 min (record for every 10 min) and (d) fluorescence variation in different concentrations of NaCl from 0.1 M to 1.0 M.

(a) Effect of different metal ions in aqueous solution on the fluorescent intensity of FCDs, (b) fluorescence spectra of FCDs in the absence (blue line) and presence (red line) of 50 μM Cu2+, (c) fluorescence spectral quenching of FCDs upon addition of various concentration of Cu2+ and (d) relative fluorescence response of FCDs (F0/F) versus concentration of Cu2+ from 0 to 50 μM. Inset is the linear region from 0 to 5 μM. F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of FCDs at 312 nm in the absence and presence of Cu2+. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Design matrix for three variables (actual and coded levels) with the observed and predicted values of quantum yield.
Sum of squares and p-values for the regression coefficients of the quadratic model with all quadratic terms.
ANOVA results for the quadratic model.

head.ce:title: Highly fluorescent carbon dots from peanut shells as potential probes for copper ion: The optimization and analysis of the synthetic process

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Soil Environment and Pollution Remediation, Institute of Soil Science, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Nanjing 210008, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Soil Environment and Pollution Remediation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Key Laboratory of Soil Environment and Pollution Remediation, Institute of Soil Science, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Nanjing 210008, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Soil Environment and Pollution Remediation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The use of natural materials, a renewable resource, instead of chemicals as carbon precursors for simple synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots (FCDs) remains a significant challenge. Here, we report the preparation of FCDs with a photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield (QY) of 10.58% from peanut shells via one-pot pyrolysis treatment optimized by using a central composite experiment design. Optimum pyrolysis conditions were found to be 400 °C temperature, 4 h duration, and 70 g peanut shell weight. The as-prepared FCDs possess unique excitation-dependent behavior, good water dispersibility and high photostability. The results of Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy to analyze the pyrolytic process indicated the complete combustion of peanut shells happened at 3 and 4 h at 400 °C. The PL intensity of the FCDs was not always proportional to the corresponding QY value in our work due to the different amount of carbon-rich residues after the pyrolysis process. Fluorescence-quenching trials were conducted to analyze their sensitivity and selectivity in Cu2+ detection. The detection limit was found to be 4.8 μM. Our pyrolysis treatment of peanut shells for preparing FCDs is not only a green and facile method but also a means of recycling peanut shells.

ce:section.ce:para: For several decades, fluorescent carbon dots (FCDs), also known as carbon nanodots or carbon quantum dots, have attracted tremendous attention because of their unique, advantageous properties such as low toxicity [1], stable photoluminescence (PL; [2]), high aqueous solubility, and good biocompatibility [1]. FCDs have therefore been widely used in fields such as bioimaging [3], photocatalysis [4], biosensing [5], and chemical sensing [6]. Synthetic routes for FCDs can be categorized into two types, namely, top-down and bottom-up methods. The former involves breakdown of larger carbon structures such as graphite nanofiber [7] and activated carbon [8] and subsequent surface passivation using polymers [9]. The latter method involves direct carbonization or pyrolysis of carbon precursors such as glycerol [10], glucose [11], and hydrophilic organic compounds [12]. However, most of the above methods utilize many kinds of chemicals as carbon precursors or surface modification agents. Bottom-up approaches for preparing FCDs such as pyrolysis of natural materials, including leeks [13], cotton [14], oats [15], lychee seeds [16], sago waste [17], shrimp eggs [18], peanut skin [19], coconut milk [20], hair [21], konjac flour [22] and oriental plane leaf [23] have been of much interest because of their simplicity and minimal use of chemicals (Table S1).

Peanuts are one of the most important oilseed crops in China, having a cultivation area of over 5 million hectares every year [24]. The harvesting of peanuts generates a large amount of peanut shells, reaching 4.5 million metric tons annually in China [25]. Peanut shells do not have value. Most of them are arbitrarily discarded or mishandled; such wastage goes against the principle of sustainability and waste recycling. Recent reports have shown that peanut shells can be used in dye adsorption [26], salt-induced soil amendment [27], and synthesis of activated carbon [28]. Peanut shell is rich in fiber, which is a suitable carbon precursor for preparing FCDs. The groups of Yuan and Du demonstrated the utilization of the fiber-rich resources wheat straw [29] and bagasse [30] as carbon precursors for synthesis of FCDs; quantum yields (QYs) achieved in their studies are 9.2% and 9.3%, respectively. Xue et al. reported the preparation of FCDs from peanut shell by treatment at 250 °C for 2 h. The method has a QY of 9.91% and potential application in the preparation of C-dots as fluorescent probes for living-cell imaging [31]. Synthesis of FCDs from peanut shells has been reported, but the interactions of the variables pyrolysis temperature, pyrolysis time, and weight of peanut shells, which may affect pyrolysis, as well as their effects on the fluorescence QYs of the FCDs, have not been investigated. Actually, the weight of carbon precursor is a very important variable in the synthesis of FCDs, which always be ignored by researchers.

In our work, we demonstrated the use of peanut shells as a carbon source for the facile environment-friendly synthesis of water-soluble FCDs by simple one-pot pyrolysis without using any chemicals. We applied response surface methodology (RSM) to optimize the pyrolysis conditions, using the variables pyrolysis temperature, pyrolysis time, and weight of peanut shells and used Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy to analyze the pyrolytic process for producing FCDs from peanut shells. We firstly demonstrated the relationship between PL intensity and the corresponding QY value of the FCDs, and then characterized in detail the optical and physicochemical properties of the synthesized FCDs. In addition, our work also examines the potential application of the synthesized FCDs as selective probes for metal ions detection.

Peanut shells were collected from Nanjing, Jiangsu, China. Soil-free peanut shells were predried in a drying oven for 48 h at 60 °C. All chemicals involved in the study, which were of reagent grade, were used without further purification. Quinine sulfate and the metal salts CuCl2·H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, AlCl3·6H2O, BaCl2·2H2O, CaCl2, MgCl2·6H2O, CoCl2·6H2O, MnCl2·4H2O, NaCl, KCl, and LiCl·H2O, as well as reagents for pH adjustment (HCl and NaOH), were purchased from China National Medicines Corporation Ltd. (Beijing, China). Rhodamine B was obtained from Tianjin Chemical Reagent Research Institute (Tianjin, China).

FCDs were synthesized by pyrolysis of peanut shells, as illustrated in Fig. 1 . An appropriate amount of precleaned peanut shells was weighed into a ceramic evaporating pan (750 mL) and transferred to a laboratory furnace. The sample was heated at different temperatures (from 340 to 420 °C) as needed. After the heating process, the resulting black products sample were allowed cool to room temperature in a desiccator. The sample was then artificially ground to a fine powder and passed through a 100-mesh sieve to obtain a homogeneous powder. The ground sample (1.0 g) was then added to 50 mL of deionized water and dispersed by 1 h sonication to form a homogeneous black mixture. The solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove the black insoluble carbonaceous particles, purified through a dialysis membrane (molecular weight cutoff: 1 kDa) for 48 h before characterization, and then finally stored at 4 °C. The powder of FCDs was obtained by lyophilization and then redispersed in deionized water to a concentration of 0.1 mg/mL.

All fluorescence measurements were performed on a Hitachi-F4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). Excitation and emission wavelengths were each set at 5 nm. A 1 cm × 1 cm quartz cell was used, and tests were done at room temperature. UV–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded using a UV-1600 spectrophotometer (Beijing, China). The morphology and dimensions of the FCDs were examined under a JEM-2100 high-resolution transmission electron microscope operated at 200 kV (Tokyo, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were generated by a PHI 5000 VersaProbe X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Chigasaki, Japan). FTIR spectroscopy was performed on a Nicolet380 spectrometer (Boston, Massachusetts, USA). A Leici PHS-3C digital pH meter (Shanghai, China) was used to measure the pH of the aqueous solutions.

A 0.1 M H2SO4 solution of quinine sulfate (54% QY) was used as the standard [16]. The absorbances of the aqueous FCDs solution and reference sample at 350 nm were kept below 0.05. The QY of FCDs (in water) was calculated through the following equation:
(1)
φ x = φ st × (I x/I st) × (η x 2/η st 2) × (A st/A x)
where the subscripts “st” and “x” refer to quinine sulfate and FCDs, respectively; φ is the QY, I is the measured integrated emission intensity, η is the refractive index of the solvent, and A is the optical density. For an aqueous solution, η x/η st = 1.

RSM is a set of mathematical and statistical techniques widely used for analyzing engineering problems dependent on several variables. Among the different experimental designs of RSM, the central composite design (CCD) is largely employed to estimate the curvature along the response surface [32]. In this work, RSM was carried out in order to optimize the variables for the pyrolysis of peanut shells and to investigate their effects on the QY of FCDs. The important variables in our experiment were found to be the pyrolysis temperature (from 340 to 420 °C), pyrolysis time (from 1 to 4 h), and starting weight of peanut shells (60–100 g). We determined the relative fluorescence QYs of all prepared FCDs at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm by integrating emission spectra in the region 370–600 nm, using quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution as standard reference (QY = 54%).

The experimental matrix in Table 1 summarizes the performed runs. The CCD for each significant factor was established at five levels (−α, −1, 0, +1, +α; α = 1.68). The rsm package of R software [33] was used to analyze the design and to plot the response surfaces. The response surfaces were constructed by holding one of the variables constant at the center point (380 °C for pyrolysis temperature, 3 h for pyrolysis time, and 80 g for peanut shell weight) and by plotting the response as a function of the other two. Analysis of variance (ANOVA) was used to examine the adequacy of the developed model. The quadratic regression model equation for the independent and dependent variables is as follows:
(2)
Y = a + bX 1 + cX 2 + dX 3 + eX 1 2 + fX 2 2 + gX 3 2 + hX 1 X 2 + iX 1 X 3 + jX 2 X 3
where Y is the QY, a is the constant coefficient, b, c and d are the coefficients for the linear, e, f and g are the coefficients for quadratic, h, i and j are the coefficients for interaction effect, X 1, X 2, and X 3 are the pyrolysis temperature, pyrolysis time, and weight of peanut shells, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH adjustment of FCD aqueous solutions
ce:section.ce:para: The pH of the FCD aqueous solutions was adjusted with 1.0 M HCl and NaOH solutions.

Solutions of chloride salts of the different metal ions were prepared. The solutions were diluted with deionized water to obtain a final concentration of 50 μM. One milliliter of FCDs solution was added to 1 mL of each metal ion solution, and the resulting mixture was stirred and stored for 10 min at room temperature. The fluorescence intensity and spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 312 nm. The relative fluorescence response of FCDs, that is, the F 0/F ratio (where F and F 0 are the fluorescence intensities of FCDs at 413 nm in the presence and absence of Cu2+, respectively), was used to evaluate the fluorescence quenching efficiency. All measurements were conducted in triplicate, and the standard deviation was indicated using error bars.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pyrolysis conditions on the QY of FCDs
ce:section.ce:para:
RSM explores the relationships between several explanatory variables and one or more response variables on the basis of fit of a polynomial equation to the experimental data. Herein, we applied RSM to analyze how the independent variables pyrolysis temperature, pyrolysis time, and peanut shell weight interact and how these interactions impact the overall response. Analysis of the data showed that the quadratic model is an appropriate empirical model of the relationship between the three variables and observed QYs. Corresponding ANOVA results for the quadratic model are given in Table 2 . Only the terms for which p < 0.05 were used to construct the model equations. The results clearly show that the two main effects (X 1, X 1 2, X 3, and X 3 2) and the binary interaction of pyrolysis temperature and pyrolysis time (X 1 X 2) are the terms with the largest effect; they were consequently included in the final model with the following regression equation.
(3) QY = 257.77 + 1.04X 1 + 1.55X 3 + 0.44X 1 X 2 − 0.0013 × 1 2 − 0.0058 × 3 2

The measured and the predicted QYs may be compared in Table 1 (last column). Fig. 2 indicates that the predicted values of the responses from the models agree well with the observed values (R 2 = 0.9727). The ANOVA results for this model are presented in Table 3 . The result R 2 = 0.9728 suggests a good fit of the data to the empirical model and thus the suitability of the model for optimization of pyrolysis variables.

Three-dimensional plots of the regression models for the interactions and their respective contour plots (Fig. 3 ) were obtained on the basis of the effects of the three independent variable at three levels. The QYs of FCDs obtained with 80 g of peanut shell are plotted as a function of pyrolysis time and temperature in Fig. 3a. A low p value (0.0115) shows a significant interaction between pyrolysis time and temperature. Fig. 3b depicts the interaction of pyrolysis temperature and peanut shell weight at 3 h of pyrolysis time. The maximum value of the response in its response surface plot has a peak suggesting that the optimum pyrolysis temperature and peanut shell weight at 3 h of pyrolysis time are approximately 400 °C and 70 g, respectively. We then further optimized the pyrolysis time from 1 to 4 h at 400 °C pyrolysis temperature and 70 g peanut shell weight. QYs follow the order 4 h > 3 h > 2 h > 1 h. Therefore, 400 °C temperature, 4 h time, and 70 g peanut shell weight are the optimum pyrolysis conditions in this study. All subsequent experiments were thus based on the optimum pyrolysis conditions. The optimum pyrolysis temperature (400 °C) is consistent with a report by Tan et al. (2014), who concluded the same optimum temperature for preparing carbon dots from sago waste. They recommended pyrolysis temperatures between 300 and 400 °C; temperatures above 400 °C may lead to complete thermal decomposition of sago waste to ashes, whereas temperatures below 300 °C may lead to incomplete combustion [17]. A high p value (0.1151) indicates fewer interactions between pyrolysis time and peanut shell weight corresponding to the response surface at 380 °C pyrolysis temperature (Fig. 3c). To evaluate the precision and reliability of the pyrolysis method, the relative standard deviation (RSD) was calculated. The RSD was obtained from three replicates of the experiments on the determination of the QYs under the same pyrolysis conditions. All RSDs are <5% (Table S2), implying that the pyrolysis method is reliable.

Fig. 4 displays UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the as-prepared FCDs. The UV absorption profile of the FCDs (Fig. 4a) is featureless, having a wide absorption band instead of a visible absorption peak. The PL spectrum of the FCDs shows a 413 nm emission peak (Fig. 4a) under the strongest excitation at 312 nm and thus the high QY (10.58%). The full width at half maximum at 312 nm is ∼90 nm, which is consistent with previous studies [30]. Fig. 4b shows the unique excitation dependence of PL of the FCDs. PL spectra were obtained at different excitation wavelengths under optimum pyrolysis conditions. That is, the emission peak shifted to longer wavelengths and the fluorescence intensity markedly decreased as the excitation wavelengths changed from 262 to 402 nm. Mechanisms related to different size distributions and the existence of different emissive trap sites on the surfaces of FCDs have been proposed to explain this unique optical phenomenon. However, they remain unclear because of lack of solid evidence [34,35].

To evaluate the relationship between fluorescence intensities and QYs of the as-prepared FCDs, PL spectra of the FCDs solution under different pyrolysis conditions were obtained (Fig. S1). Interestingly, the PL intensity of the FCDs was not always proportional to the corresponding QYs in our work. Although the strongest PL intensity was obtained at 360 °C temperature, 4 h time, and 70 g peanut shell weight, the highest QY was obtained under the optimum pyrolysis conditions of 400 °C, 4 h, and 70 g (Fig. S1). In short, the pyrolysis treatment with the highest QY did not always result in the strongest PL intensity. This phenomenon may be attributed to the low amount of carbon-rich residues after the pyrolysis process, that is, the residual weight fraction (RWF). The RWF is computed as RWF = (M/M 0) × 100, where M and M 0 are the dry masses of peanut shells respectively after and before pyrolysis treatment under the same conditions. As seen in Table S3, at 400 °C pyrolysis temperature and 70 g peanut shell weight, pyrolysis times ranked according to the resulting RWFs follow the order 1 h > 2 h > 3 h > 4 h. Likewise, at 400 °C pyrolysis temperature and 4 h pyrolysis time, peanut shell weights ranked according to the resulting RWFs follow the order 90 g > 70 g (Table S3). These results indicate that a relatively long pyrolysis time and lower peanut shell weight results in lower RWF or higher conversion rate of peanut shells. A lower RWF indicates more peanut shells decomposing into ashes instead of forming carbon-rich residues.

Fig. 5 shows low and high-magnification images and the diameter distribution of the FCDs obtained by transmission electron microscopy and the corresponding XRD pattern of the FCDs. Fig. 5a–b clearly shows that the as-synthesized FCDs have uniform size and a nearly spherical shape. Under optimum pyrolysis conditions, carbonization of peanut shells formed FCDs with an average diameter of 3.3 nm and size of 1.8–4.2 nm (Fig. 5c). XRD pattern depicts a broad amorphous peak at 2θ = 23.6° along with a weak diffraction peak (d = 3.1 Å) at 2θ = 28.5° attributing to the [002] spacing of graphitic carbon (Fig. 5d) [36].

In order to determine the chemical composition of the FCDs, they were analyzed by XPS. Dominant peaks at 284 and 531 eV in the XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 6 a) are attributed to C1s and O1s, which suggest the existence of carbon and oxygen. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 6b) displays peaks at 284.5, 285.7 and 288.2 eV, which suggest that the FCDs have C=C, C–O, and C=O groups, respectively. Curves fitted to the O1s spectra contain peaks at 531.7 and 532.6 eV, which are associated with C=O and C–O (Fig. 6c), respectively, in good agreement with the C1s spectrum.

FTIR spectra were recorded to determine the chemical composition of the prepared FCDs. To investigate the carbonization and decomposition of peanut shells into carbon-rich residues under optimum conditions, we obtained FTIR absorption spectra of the shells, of peanut shells treated at 400 °C for 1, 2, 3, and 4 h, and of synthesized FCDs (Fig. 6d–i). Absorption spectra of peanut shells and of shells treated at 1 and 2 h generally show similar characteristic absorption peaks (Fig. 6d–f), indicating the presence of similar functional groups. This result suggests that the combustion of peanut shells at 2 h is incomplete and thus requires longer heating time. In detail, the peak at 1429 cm−1 in Fig. 6d–f is caused by CH2 bending [26], and that at 1590 cm−1 is assigned to aromatic C=C stretching vibration [10]. Bands at 1262 cm−1 and 3440 cm−1 are respectively attributed to O–H in-plane bending and O–H stretching vibrations, which imply the existence of large numbers of hydroxyl groups from macromolecules such as cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin [10,26]. However, FTIR spectra of shells treated at 3 and 4 h show many differences (Fig. 6g and h), suggesting the formation of different functional groups. For 3 h and 4 h treatment, new bands at 1020–1160 cm−1 was attributed to the formation of C–O stretching vibration [36]. With 4 h of pyrolysis time, the intensity of the peak at 1590 cm−1 increased and the peak shifted to 1630 cm−1 (C=C stretching vibration) and a new band at 1739 cm−1 was attributed to the formation of carboxyl groups (C=O) [10,37]. The results indicate the complete combustion of peanut shells at 3 h and 4 h at 400 °C, which is consistent with RSM data. The FTIR spectra for the FCDs prepared under optimum pyrolysis conditions are shown in Fig. 6i. The intensity of the peak at 3400 cm−1 decreased and the peak shifted to 3545 and 3408 cm−1 (O–H stretching vibrations). Peaks at 1020–1160 cm−1 and 1650–1900 cm−1 are attributed to C–O and C=O stretching vibrations, respectively [36,37]. In addition, the absorption band for C=C stretching vibration appeared at 1620–1680 cm−1. These functional groups are similar to those of FCDs prepared in other studies [10,18,37]. Some differences in the FTIR spectrum for FCDs (i) compared with that for peanut shells treated at 4 h (h) can be observed. The reasons may be explained by the fact that differences of physicochemical components and contents between FCDs and pyrolysis product. FCDs are the water-soluble components of pyrolysis product whereas pyrolysis sample treated at 4 h are a mixture of black pyrolysis product with minor ashy content, mainly including hydrocarbons, phenol, cresols and additional heavier products [17]. As the heating time was raised from 1 to 4 h, the corresponding intensity of FTIR spectral peaks increased and reached the maximum value at 4 h of heating. This increase suggests that the proportions of C–O and C=O increased and that more oxygen-containing groups formed on the surface of FCDs, in agreement with the XPS results. The presence of these functional groups also suggests that the synthesized FCDs have high water solubility. Results of structure characterization thus indicate that FCDs were successfully synthesized from peanut shells by one-step pyrolysis treatment.

In order to assess the photostability of FCDs prepared under the optimum pyrolysis conditions, we evaluated the stability of their fluorescence intensity in aqueous solution in various experiments. Fig. 7 a indicates that the FCDs have high photostability; the PL intensity of the FCDs changed little after continuous UV irradiation for 120 min, whereas that of conventional organic dyes (rhodamine B) decreased, consistent with previous studies [30]. As the temperature increased from −4 to 60 °C, the PL intensity of the as-prepared FCDs decreased by 25% (Fig. 7b), in accordance with other reports [38,39]. The decrease in PL intensity may be attributed to the aggregation of the FCDs [38] and the non-radiative energy transfer [40].

The pH dependence of FCDs was also studied. The fluorescence intensity of FCDs solutions decreased sharply at pH 3.0 and increased slightly at pH 11.0 (Fig. 7c). This pH-dependent behavior is in accordance with that of FCDs synthesized from coriander leaves by one-step hydrothermal treatment [41]. Sachdev and Gopinath (2015) proposed that this behavior may arise from changes in the surface state of FCDs due to ionization of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. Although the fluorescence intensity of FCDs in our work steadily increased as the pH changed from 4.0 to 10.0, it was almost unchanged at pH 7.0–8.0, the range for bioimaging applications. The PL intensities of the FCDs in NaCl solution of different concentrations were recorded to analyze the stability of FCDs under high-ionic-strength environments. As shown in Fig. 7d, the PL intensity changed little, revealing that FCDs are stable in such environments. The above findings suggest that the FCDs have great potential in sensing applications under physiological conditions.

In recent years, various research groups have developed fluorescence-based sensors for selective and sensitive detection of metal ions on the basis of interactions of oxygen functional groups on the surface of FCDs with metal ions, which form complexes. Therefore, the FCDs have potential use as fluorescent probe for the detection of metal ions. In order to investigate the potential use of FCDs obtained from peanut shells in analytical methods, their fluorescence intensity in the presence of the metal ions was monitored. Fig. 8 a depicts the change in the fluorescence intensity of FCDs in the presence of various metal ions. The PL intensity obviously weakened after addition of Cu2+ to the FCDs solution. This distinct effect of Cu2+ may be attributed to the chelation of Cu2+ by FCDs via nitrogen and oxygen functional groups on their surfaces [42]. Furthermore, the visual fluorescence photographs of FCDs' solution with different concentration of Cu2+ from 10−2–105 μM was given (Fig. S2). As shown in Fig. 8b, the fluorescence intensity of the FCDs at 413 nm decreased to ∼48% upon addition of 50 μM Cu2+. The fluorescence quenching of FCDs at Cu2+ concentrations of 0–50 μM is depicted in Fig. 8c. Upon the addition of Cu2+, the PL intensity gradually decreased, implying that the PL intensity of the mixture is sensitive to the Cu2+ concentration. The relative fluorescence response of FCDs (i.e., F 0/F ratio) is plotted a function of Cu2+ concentration in terms of the Stern–Volmer equation in Fig. 8d. The calibration curve shows a linear relationship (R 2 = 0.9916) between the F 0/F ratio and Cu2+ concentration over Cu2+ concentrations of 0–5 μM (inset in Fig. 8d). The linear equation is as follows:
(4)
F 0/F = 0.0781X + 0.9972
where F 0 and F are, respectively, the fluorescence intensities of FCDs in the absence and presence of Cu2+, and X is Cu2+ concentration. The calculation was based on 3σ/s, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank signal (n = 3), and s is the slope of the linear curve derived from the Stern–Volmer equation [43]. From the above equation, the limit of detection (LOD) of the FCDs for Cu2+ sensing was found to be 4.8 μM. This LOD is lower than those of previously reported methods [17,44]. Taken together, the aforementioned results suggest a promising method for assaying Cu2+.

Water-soluble FCDs derived from peanut shells was prepared via a simple and green pyrolysis method without using any chemicals. A CCD was adopted in this study to determine the optimal experimental conditions for pyrolysis. The optimum conditions for this pyrolysis process were found to be 400 °C pyrolysis temperature, 4 h pyrolysis time, and 70 g of peanut shell weight. The aqueous solution of synthesized FCDs strongly emits blue-green light under UV irradiation, having a fluorescence QY of 10.58%. The prepared FCDs have a narrow size distribution, 3.3 nm average particle size, good optical characteristics and photostability. They can also be used as a fluorescence sensor for detecting Cu2+, which extends the range of their use. The present work can therefore provide a new one-pot pyrolysis method for preparing FCDs from other agricultural wastes such as sunflower seeds shell, corncob, and crop straw, which we plan to carry out in future.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was funded by Strategic Priority Research Program of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (NO. XDB15030301) and Knowledge Innovation Program of the Institute of Soil Science, Chinese Academy of Sciences (Grant No. ISSASIP1634).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Tables S1–S3 & Figs. S1 and S2 Tables S1–S3 & Figs. S1 and S2

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.mtchem.2017.04.004.
